title: Gymnosporia ambigua
intro: Gymnosporia ambigua, also known as African quinine or bitter-bush, is a plant species belonging to the family Celastraceae, characterized by its woody branches and small, white or yellowish flowers. The plant has been traditionally used in African medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and malaria, due to its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antimalarial properties. The bioactive constituents of Gymnosporia ambigua include flavonoids, tannins, and alkaloids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Gymnosporia ambigua is often prepared as a decoction or infusion, and it is also used in the form of a tea or a tincture to treat various health ailments.
taxonomy: Gymnosporia ambigua is classified in the family Celastraceae. This family is a part of the larger order Celastrales. The order Celastrales belongs to the class Magnoliopsida, which is a group of flowering plants. The class Magnoliopsida is a part of the division Magnoliophyta, also known as angiosperms. The division Magnoliophyta is a major group within the kingdom Plantae.
